title: The Platformization of the Public Sphere and its Challenge to Democracy
author: Fischer and Jarren

opening_move.description: Digital transformation has disrupted the public sphere's social mediation function: journalistic news values relevant to democracy are being replaced by platform attention-seeking business logic, fragmenting the public sphere and undermining democratic orientation.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A broad public debate about platformization and possible policies is urgently needed to ensure a media system that serves and strengthens democracy.

rivals_engaged: optimistic early internet accounts (everyone can participate)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper extends Habermasian public sphere theory to the digital platform era. It diagnoses platformization as a continuation of the structural transformation of the public sphere — journalism's social mediation function is being replaced by platform algorithmic logic — and calls for regulatory response.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Analysis of media industry trends, platform business models, journalism crisis indicators (illustrative).
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Habermasian public sphere analysis applied to digital platforms; political economy of platforms.
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper references empirical trends in journalism decline and platformization but does not conduct original empirical research. Evidence is illustrative of the theoretical argument. Classified as normative_with_qualitative_empirical (illustrative) — the call for regulation is normatively derived, not empirically grounded.

case_type.case_name: Platformization of journalism and the public sphere

normative_stake.domain: public sphere / journalism / platform regulation / democracy

confidence_notes: Type 5c (illustrative diagnosis): the Habermasian framework drives the argument; platformization evidence illustrates rather than constitutes. This paper reads like a policy-oriented contribution to a special issue — less philosophically dense than most PSC papers, closer to media studies.
